name: Maya, The Independent Design Consultant
summary: Maya is a highly skilled, efficiency-minded freelance designer who manages multiple client brands simultaneously. She views time as her most costly resource and seeks tools that automate repetitive tasks (like template setup and asset sharing) and allow her to operate seamlessly without expensive per-seat licensing models. She needs maximum control and deep integration.
goals: Streamline the client onboarding experience to reduce setup time.
Maintain brand consistency across multiple, disparate client projects.
Scale her client capacity from 1 to 15+ without increasing operational costs linearly.
Provide a seamless, non-technical presentation view for her end-users.
pains: Wasting time on manual, repetitive design setup and asset compilation.
Licensing structures (per-seat pricing) that negatively impact solo, multi-client businesses.
Having non-technical clients confused or frustrated by complex editing interfaces.
Lack of control over digital assets and client-facing workflows (e.g., not being able to truly brand the output).
motivations: Maximizing billable hours by eliminating design friction.
Building a 'system' that can handle rapid client turnover.
The perceived value of time savings (e.g., saving 10 minutes per project).
Professional recognition as a reliable, seamless brand partner.
objections: High initial cost or mandatory per-user licensing fees.
Over-engineered features that confuse the primary use case (i.e., complex features she won't use).
Poor white-labeling capability that results in visible product branding.
decision_triggers: Viewing a specific workflow improvement (a 'how-to' video) that saves visible time.
A vendor offering a 'power user' or 'agency' licensing tier.
Recommendations from other successful independent studios.
sample_quotes: I bill clients hourly, so anything that saves me 10 minutes per project is worth real money.
My clients are non-technical. They get confused by your whole edit interface.
I love the templates, honestly. It saves me hours when starting a new client brand.
Can your client share view be white-labeled? I really need that.

journey_stages.mindset: Seeking efficiency gains and scalable structure.
content_preferences: Technical deep dives focusing on API integration and customization (developer documentation).

journey_stages.mindset: Evaluating long-term value vs. short-term cost.
key_actions: Confirming the ability to truly rebrand the whole platform.

source_evidence.claim: Wants to maximize billable hours by eliminating design friction and valuing time savings.
Faces difficulty with traditional per-seat licensing models due to operating as a solo, multi-client business.
Requires the ability to white-label client-facing views and outputs.
Prioritizes tools that automate asset generation, branding, and templates to improve efficiency.
Identifies a pain point with overly complex interfaces for non-technical end-users.

backstory: Honestly, I got into brand consulting because I realized creative vision is really only half the fight. I spent the early years of my career constantly wrestling with inconsistent brand guidelines and terrible hand-off processes. I built my practice working independently, which was great for creative control, but man, the operational side? It was a nightmare. I started billing clients hourly to pay the bills, but I was burning out on the repetitive tasks—the asset exporting, the template setup, the client sharing. Now, I'm obsessed with finding 'the system' that let me focus 100% on design, not data entry.
speech_patterns: Starts sentences with a quantification of effort or time: 'Honestly, it saves me hours when...', 'I bill clients hourly so...'
Uses hedging/qualifiers when discussing technical limitations: 'I think there should be...', 'Probably we need to...'
Frequent use of the discourse marker: 'That said...'
Emphasizes professional process: 'Brand governance,' 'Operational scope,' 'Client-side view.'
emotional_triggers: When she discovers unnecessary manual work (e.g., spending a day fixing a template instead of designing something new).
When a platform forces her to buy more seats when her client count is high but her team size is one.
When a client struggles with a platform because the interface is too geared toward advanced content creators, not visual thinkers.
tone: Highly Professional and Efficient, uses sophisticated industry jargon while maintaining a slightly impatient edge when discussing wasted time.
